one_line_claim: Fast-Slow Training argues that LLM post-training should co-optimize slow model weights with fast textual prompt populations, so task-specific feedback can move into context instead of being forced entirely into RL-updated parameters.
what_they_tested: The authors interleave CISPO/GRPO-style RLVR updates with GEPA prompt evolution on Qwen3-8B-family models, evaluating CodeIO, Polaris math, HoVer-hard, Physics, a synthetic star-graph task, a two-phase plasticity probe, and a HoVer -> CodeIO -> Physics continual-learning stream. They compare mainly against parameter-only RL, plus Base/RL/FST followed by GEPA for some generalization checks, GEPA-only and FST-distill on HoVer, and CodeIO design ablations for prompt-pool size, advantage grouping, cycle length, and GEPA recipe.
strongest_point: The strongest evidence is the coherent diagnostic chain: FST reaches RL's running peak in 1.4-3x fewer training steps on three tasks, has higher fitted asymptotes, sits on a better reward/KL frontier on most tasks, and the design ablations show that population size, per-problem advantage grouping, short cycles, and incremental GEPA edits matter.
weakest_point: The main weakness is that the headline sample-efficiency and continual-learning claims are not compute- or information-matched: FST adds repeated GEPA rollouts, gpt-5.2 reflection calls, lookahead-batch prompt optimization, and sometimes cached trajectories, while the baseline is mostly plain RL rather than a budget-matched suite of conservative RL, replay, adapters, prompt-tuned RL, or other continual-learning methods.
missing_baselines: Compute-matched and wall-clock-matched RLVR that spends the same H100 time, rollout budget, and reflection/API budget as FST, not just the same or fewer optimizer steps.
Sequential prompt-then-RL and RL-then-prompt baselines run with the same GEPA budget, including fixed GEPA prompts during RL, to isolate co-evolution from simply finding a better system prompt.
RL with a manually tuned or GEPA-derived prompt pool but no ongoing prompt evolution, since the CodeIO ablations show even K=1 optimized prompts help.
Conservative or plasticity-preserving RL baselines such as stronger KL regularization, entropy bonuses, replay, reset/reinit tricks, LoRA/adapters, or EWC-style continual-learning controls.
Fast-only and slow-only baselines on all main domains, not just HoVer, including GEPA-only with equal metric calls and FST-distill across CodeIO, Math, and Physics.
A rich-feedback baseline that converts rollout critiques into supervised data or reward shaping without maintaining a GEPA prompt population.
Model-family and scale baselines beyond Qwen3-8B and the custom Polaris SFT base, including smaller/larger instruction-tuned models and non-Qwen models.
missing_ablations: Independent training seeds and confidence intervals for the full RL/FST runs; the paper shows many curves but not retraining variance for a noisy, multi-stage recipe.
GEPA reflection-model sensitivity, including smaller open models, older OpenAI models, no-reflection variants, and cost/quality tradeoffs for gpt-5.2.
GEPA budget sweeps on all domains: metric calls, anchor-set size, number of candidate prompts, proposer prompt, and whether the anchor/lookahead batch is also used for RL.
Holdout-anchor versus lookahead-batch prompt optimization, to test whether FST is benefiting from extra pre-optimization on exactly the next RL batches.
Prompt population diversity metrics and best-prompt versus sampled-population comparisons throughout training, not only endpoint examples of evolved prompts.
Per-domain ablations for K, cycle length, advantage grouping, and light/full GEPA; Appendix E reports these only on CodeIO.
Retention and forgetting measurements on previous tasks and broad capability suites after task switches, not only new-task acquisition or KL distance to the base.
Absolute continual-learning curves and unnormalized task accuracies after each switch; normalized-to-peak plots make it harder to judge real retention and task tradeoffs.
red_flags: The paper says FST is up to 3x sample-efficient, but the comparison is mainly in optimizer steps while FST performs extra GEPA evaluations, reflection calls, and prompt selection between RL updates.
End-to-end FST is explicitly more expensive than RL-only at equal step count; the appendix says periodic GEPA cycles add real wall-clock on top and use a paid gpt-5.2 reflection model.
The fast loop pre-fetches the next T RL batches and optimizes prompts on that lookahead batch before the RL phase, so the method gets richer access to upcoming training examples than plain RL.
The catastrophic-forgetting claim is mostly proxied by KL-to-base and second-task learnability; the paper does not show broad retention of original capabilities or previous trained tasks.
The continual-learning result uses one three-task stream with task switches every 200 steps and normalized validation accuracy, without replay/adapters/EWC/reset baselines or seed variance.
The Polaris KL result is an exception: the appendix says RL and FST sit on top of each other in KL/reward space because the custom SFT base follows GEPA prompts poorly.
The OOD/generalization check applies GEPA after both RL and FST checkpoints, which makes it harder to attribute downstream generalization gains to the trained slow weights alone.
Evolved prompts are long negative-example checklists built from observed failures; this is useful engineering but may overfit to benchmark-specific verifier and formatting quirks.
positive_signals: The fast/slow formulation is concrete: slow weights are updated by RLVR, while fast textual weights are a Pareto-frontier prompt population optimized from rollout traces and feedback.

The evaluation spans code output prediction, math, multi-hop fact verification, Physics, a synthetic exploration task, plasticity probes, and a continual task stream, which is broader than a single benchmark win.

The authors measure more than endpoint accuracy: they report matched-step efficiency, fitted asymptotes, KL-to-base reward frontiers, second-task learnability, entropy, and wall-clock effects of rollout reuse.
The CodeIO design ablations are informative: K=8, per-problem advantage grouping, short T=6 cycles, and the light incremental GEPA recipe all matter.
The paper is unusually candid about cost: it gives H100 setup, per-step wall-clock, GEPA metric-call budgets, and the fact that GEPA cycles are a sizable extra cost.
The star-graph experiment makes the exploration story intuitive by showing fast prompts escaping a zero-reward regime much earlier than parameter-only RL.
The rollout-reuse appendix is practical and falsifiable: cached GEPA trajectories reduce HoVer RL-step wall time while tracking non-reuse FST accuracy.
verdict: Worth covering. The paper has a strong central idea for the current RLVR moment: do not make model weights memorize every task-specific lesson when prompts can absorb fast feedback. The empirical package is substantial and podcast-friendly, especially the fast/slow framing, zero-reward star graph, and plasticity story. But the review should be blunt that the main wins are not yet compute-matched, rely on a powerful external prompt optimizer, use mostly plain RL baselines, and only weakly establish catastrophic forgetting or general continual learning.
citations: data/papers/arxiv-2605.12484.md:1-22 identifies the title, metadata, arXiv date, and abstract claims of up to 3x sample efficiency, up to 70% lower KL, reduced forgetting, plasticity, and continual adaptation.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:71-76 describes Figure 1: FST jointly optimizes slow parameters and a fast textual context pool, with the fast loop consuming rollout text, tool calls, errors, and feedback.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:85-91 states the core co-evolution claim: RL updates are interleaved with GEPA context optimization rather than prompt-tuning after the final checkpoint.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:166-173 explains GEPA as a reflective prompt evolution procedure using a frozen reflection LM and a Pareto frontier of candidate prompts.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:188-198 defines the FST training cycle: pre-fetch the next T RL batches, run GEPA with the current policy and reflection LM, then update weights while sampling each prompt in the rollout group.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:206-216 reports the main in-distribution setup and matched-step results: CodeIO, Polaris, and HoVer-hard, mostly Qwen3-8B, with FST reaching RL's running peak in 3.0x, 1.4x, and 3.0x fewer steps.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:301-309 summarizes Figure 2, including the FST -> GEPA comparisons on in-distribution, cross-domain, and easy-to-hard axes.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:310-322 reports fitted performance asymptotes: FST exceeds RL by +4.4 points on CodeIO, +2.9 on Math, and +7.7 on HoVer-hard.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:367-386 reports the reward/KL and plasticity claims, including FST's lower KL frontier and Math/Physics -> HoVer-hard second-phase results.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:388-432 describes the HoVer -> CodeIO -> Physics continual-learning stream and reports the CodeIO stage gap: RL peaks at 20.7 mean@16 while FST finishes at 37.7.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:438-450 reports the synthetic star-graph result where FST reaches measurable reward around step 50 while RL stays near zero for roughly 300 steps.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:493-501 reports the HoVer component comparison: GEPA-only is below slow-weight methods, FST-distill trails reward-optimizing slow weights, and FST has the highest final ceiling.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:534-540 lists the authors' stated future work: changing prompt/weight optimizers, improving compute efficiency, trajectory reuse, and broader distillation study.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:993-1012 gives shared RL and GEPA hyperparameters, including GRPO/CISPO settings, G=8 rollouts per problem, mean@4 evaluation, T=6 GEPA cycles, and gpt-5.2 reflection through LiteLLM.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:1014-1047 gives compute details: 8x H100 runs, RL-only around 60 seconds per step, FST without reuse around 100 seconds per RL step on HoVer-hard, plus extra GEPA cycle wall-clock and gpt-5.2 costs.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:1048-1190 reports CodeIO design ablations: K sweeps, prompt versus problem baselines, T=6 versus T=12, light versus full GEPA, and the 42.84% K=8 headline ablation result.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:1191-1220 explains rollout reuse and reports a HoVer-hard speedup from about 66 seconds to 47 seconds per RL step with no observed accuracy cost.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:1253-1267 states that on Polaris, RL and FST collapse onto each other in KL/reward space because the custom SFT base has weaker instruction following and benefits less from GEPA prompts.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12484.txt:1268-1284 explains that evolved prompts are co-evolved with RL and often become long negative-example-specific checklists based on low-reward rollout failures.
